coredata.dc:title: Tunable luminescence and morphological evolution of facile synthesized zinc borate/carbon dots composites for NUV-WLEDs
coredata.dc:creator: He, Lili
Bai, Yufeng
Ge, Chunhua
Yang, Huizhi
Yu, Xiaohan
Zhang, Xiangdong
coredata.dc:description: A series of zinc borate/carbon dots composites (ZBH/yNCDs) were successfully synthesized under hydrothermal conditions. The influences of yellow N-doped carbon dots (yNCDs) content on the structure, morphologies and luminescence properties of the zinc borate (4ZnO·B2O3·H2O, ZBH) were investigated. With increasing yNCDs content, ZBH in the ZBH/yNCDs composites gradually lost water and eventually became ZB/yNCDs (4ZnO·B2O3, ZB) composites. Meanwhile, the tunable luminescence of ZBH/yNCDs composites and the morphological evolution from rod-like to block to sphere-like were achieved by varying the yNCDs content in the zinc borate matrix. The results revealed that the ZBH/yNCDs composite with sphere-like morphology has the strongest white light emission at 395 nm excitation wavelength. Therefore, the ZBH/yNCDs composites may be used as phosphors in the near-ultraviolet (NUV) excitation white-light illumination field. The discovery of this work may also provide new inspirations for solving the self-quenching effect of carbon dots in the solid-state display applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Zinc borate
Morphology
White light-emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The relationship between dopant concentration, morphology and PL intensity of phosphors

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of the Education Department of Liaoning Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of the Education Department of Liaoning Province ( LJC201916 and LFW201901 ). The author would also like to thank everyone involved in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of yNCDs and a series of ZBH/yNCDs (0-7) samples.
XRD patterns of yNCDs and a series ZBH/yNCDs (0-7) samples.
SEM images of a series of ZBH/yNCDs (0-7) samples.
Selected EDS spectra of ZBH/yNCDs (5). The inset shows the elements mapping images.

(a) PL emission spectra of a series of ZBH/yNCDs (0-7) samples. (b) The dependence of emission intensity on yNCDs content. (c) CIE chromaticity diagram of ZBH/yNCDs (0-7) and yNCDs samples are recorded at 395 nm excitation. The inset shows optical images of WLEDs devices.
PL excitation spectra of ZBH/yNCDs (5) under 448 nm and 553 nm emission wavelengths, respectively.
Schematic diagram of the possible formation process of ZBH/yNCDs.

head.ce:title: Tunable luminescence and morphological evolution of facile synthesized zinc borate/carbon dots composites for NUV-WLEDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Chongshan Road No. 66, Shenyang, 110036, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Chongshan Road No. 66, Shenyang, 110036, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of zinc borate/carbon dots composites (ZBH/yNCDs) were successfully synthesized under hydrothermal conditions. The influences of yellow N-doped carbon dots (yNCDs) content on the structure, morphologies and luminescence properties of the zinc borate (4ZnO·B2O3·H2O, ZBH) were investigated. With increasing yNCDs content, ZBH in the ZBH/yNCDs composites gradually lost water and eventually became ZB/yNCDs (4ZnO·B2O3, ZB) composites. Meanwhile, the tunable luminescence of ZBH/yNCDs composites and the morphological evolution from rod-like to block to sphere-like were achieved by varying the yNCDs content in the zinc borate matrix. The results revealed that the ZBH/yNCDs composite with sphere-like morphology has the strongest white light emission at 395 nm excitation wavelength. Therefore, the ZBH/yNCDs composites may be used as phosphors in the near-ultraviolet (NUV) excitation white-light illumination field. The discovery of this work may also provide new inspirations for solving the self-quenching effect of carbon dots in the solid-state display applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) have gradually replaced usual incandescent and fluorescent lamps owing to the unique advantages in high energy efficiency, long operational life, remarkable energy saving, safety, and environmental protection, known as the fourth-generation light source [1–3]. Currently, phosphor-converted WLEDs are the mainstream type of solid-state light source. Two typical configurations of WLEDs are accomplished by the combination of a blue LED chip and yellow phosphors, or ultraviolet (UV) LED chip and three primary colors (blue, green, and red) phosphors [4–6]. However, they may encounter some serious issues, such as the low color rendering index of devices, the complex process of preparing and encapsulating a variety of phosphors, the possible color changes on account of the different degradation rates of each component. The latest near-ultraviolet (NUV) LED chips to excite a white phosphor is the most promising because it simplifies the device structure, eliminates the self-absorption and color instability of multiple color combinations, and has practical application value. Therefore, it is a unique and challenging task to design a new phosphor that directly generates white light emission and is excited by the NUV chip [7–9]. Furthermore, WLEDs are still relatively expensive so far [10,11]. The price of WLEDs is ten times that of incandescent lamps and twice that of compact fluorescent lamps. This is because the synthesis of phosphors usually requires high-energy processes and doping expensive rare earth metals. Also, some rare-earth ions are toxic to humans and the environment. So, it is imperative to design a novel rare earth-free phosphor with a simple synthesis process and low-cost for WLEDs [12–15].

Carbon dots (CDs) as an emerging carbon-based nanomaterial have attracted widespread attention due to low toxicity, low cost, good biocompatibility, and excellent optical properties [16–18]. Heteroatom doping and surface modification are two major ways of controlling the optical properties of CDs [19–21]. Some studies have shown that nitrogen (N) is the most typical dopant due to its atomic size comparable to that of carbon and its five valence electrons can be tightly bound to carbon atoms. In N-doped carbon dots (NCDs), the unpaired electrons of nitrogen atoms can act as electron donors to improve the optical properties of CDs, such as increasing the quantum yield and adjusting the emission wavelength [22]. In recent years, new CDs-based fluorescent materials have become the most ideal substitute for rare-earth-based expensive phosphors in WLEDs [23–26]. However, the studies of the CDs-based WLEDs are still in its infancy. At present, it is a problem with most CDs solid powders that only the very weak fluorescence on account of the self-quenching effect. The absence of solid-state luminescence greatly limits the application of CDs in WLEDs. In order to effectively alleviate the adverse aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) effects, matrix encapsulation is an effective way. Many studies have proved that CDs can be dispersed and protected by embedding solid matrices, such as borax [27], silicone [28,29], starch [30], silica xerogel [31], and epoxy resin [32,33]. In this case, the matrix acts as a water-like medium in which CDs can be evenly dispersed, so the fluorescence is almost the same as that of the aqueous solution of CDs. Therefore, finding a host matrix suitable for CDs is also critical to the development of WLEDs.

In the past few years, zinc borate has stolen the limelight by virtue of its excellent optical properties [34–36]. Zinc borates have a variety of structures because of its unique B-O chemical bond characteristics. So far, many kinds of zinc borate have been successfully synthesized in the laboratory, which includes 2ZnO·3B2O3·3H2O, 2ZnO·3B2O3·7H2O, 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O, Zn4O(BO3)2, 4ZnO·3B2O3 and so forth. Among them, 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O is considered as a new matrix material due to its low price, adjustable morphology, high chemical stability, and low synthesis temperature. In previous studies, we have successfully synthesized 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O:Ln3+ (Ln = Eu, Tb) and 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O/hydrothermal carbon microsphere (ZBH/HTCs) composites via one-step hydrothermal treatment process , which exhibit strong white emission [37,38]. It is well known that a suitable ratio of blue and yellow light will produce white light [39,40]. 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O itself possesses blue light emission. Therefore, it suggests that if yellow NCDs (yNCDs) are introduced into 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O host matrix, 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O (ZBH) not only used as the host matrix to inhibit the AIQ effects of CDs but also ZBH/yNCDs composites may obtain white light-emitting.

In the present study, a series of the ZBH/yNCDs white-light-emitting composites have been successfully synthesized through the hydrothermal method. The tunable morphology and luminescence of ZBH/yNCDs composites were achieved by varying the yNCDs content in the zinc borate matrix. The influences of yNCDs content on the morphologies and luminescence properties of the ZBH/yNCDs composites are investigated in detail, and the effects of different morphologies of ZBH/yNCDs composites on the luminescence intensity are discussed. Besides, the color chromaticity of the ZBH/yNCDs composite is also studied, which presents potential applications in NUV-WLEDs.

Yellow N-doped carbon dots (yNCDs) were synthesized by the hydrothermal reaction of O-phenylenediamine (o-PD, 99.9% purity), which is similar to previous reports [41]. 0.15 g o-PD was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water. The solution was then moved to the Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained for 5 h at 160 °C. After the reaction was over, big particles were removed from the obtained suspension by centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min. The yNCDs aqueous solution were purified by dialysis in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff: 1000 Da) for 12 h. Finally, the yellow powders were obtained by freeze-drying the yNCDs solution.

Zinc borate/carbon dots composite (ZBH/yNCDs) samples were prepared by a hydrothermal method. 0.4084 g boric acid (H3BO3, 99.9% purity) and 0.5752 g zinc vitriol (ZnSO4·7H2O, 99.9% purity) were dissolved in 8 mL deionized water by heating and stirring. The pH of the mixed solution was regulated to about 7 with ammonia water (NH3·H2O, 25 wt%), and sol-gel was obtained. A series of yNCDs were separately dispersed in deionized water, mixed with the above sol-gel and stirred for 60 min. Then they were transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave, respectively, and maintained for 72 h at 180 °C. After completion of the reaction, the solid products were isolated by filtration, washed alternately with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH, 75.0% purity) several times at atmospheric pressure. Finally, ZBH/yNCDs powder samples were obtained by vacuum drying in the oven at 60 °C for 12 h. A series of ZBH/yNCDs samples were designated as ZBH/yNCDs (0-7), corresponding to the addition of 0 g, 0.02 g, 0.04 g, 0.06 g, 0.08 g, 0.10 g, 0.12 g and 0.14 g of yNCDs, respectively. Among them, ZBH/yNCDs (0) sample is a pure ZBH.
For fabrication of WLEDs devices, ZBH/yNCDs (5) samples were mixed with silica gel and covered on commercial LED chips (395 nm, the voltage of 3.2 V), and then cured at 120 °C for 3h.

Fourier Transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), photoluminescence (PL) and PL excitation (PLE) spectroscopy was performed on these samples. FT-IR spectra (4000-400 cm−1) were measured by Nicolet AVATAR 360 spectrometer with the KBr sheet as background. XRD was performed with AXIS D8 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Ka target, voltage: 40 KV, current: 30 mA, diffraction angle: 2θ = 10-70°, scanning speed: 10°/min, etc. EOL JSM-6711F scanning electron microscope equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer was used to observe the micro-morphology and elemental compositions of the samples at 10 kV test voltage. PL, and PLE spectra were performed with an F-4500 spectrophotometer under the excitation of a xenon lamp. According to the measured PL data, the chromaticity coordinates were calculated by the CIE 1931 standard colorimetric system.

Fig. 1 shows the FT-IR spectra of yNCDs and a series of ZBH/yNCDs (0-7) samples. The yNCDs samples have a broad absorption band around 3442 cm−1, which is related to the stretching vibrations of -OH and -NH, respectively. The absorption band at 2930 cm−1 is derived from the C-H stretching vibration. Meanwhile, the absorptions at 1627, 1506 and 1126 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching modes of bond C=N/C=O, C=C, and C-O-C, respectively. The absorption band at 1378 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of C-N [42]. The above results confirm that there may be some functional groups on yNCDs surface, such as -COOH, -OH and -NH2 and so on. For ZBH/yNCDs (1-7) samples, we observed the characteristic bands of ZBH [43] and yNCDs simultaneously, indicating that ZBH/yNCDs composites can be produced by the hydrothermal method. The ZBH is the main component in the ZBH/yNCDs (0-5) composites when the yNCDs content is less. With the gradual increase of yNCDs content, the intensity of the zinc borate characteristic band becomes weaker and the intensity of the yNCDs characteristic band becomes stronger. However, it is noteworthy that when the yNCDs content reaches 0.1g, the characteristic absorption band at 684 cm−1 begins to appear in ZBH/yNCDs (5-7) samples, which corresponds to the out-of-plane bending of B(3)-O. This result indicates that the increase of yNCDs may change the structure of 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O compounds and form a new kind of zinc borate Zn4O(BO3)2 [44]. At the same time, the results were confirmed by XRD.

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of yNCDs and a series of ZBH/yNCDs (0-7) samples. A broad peak around 2θ = 21.8° can be observed from yNCDs sample (Fig. 2 (a)), corresponding to the (002) crystal face of carbon-based materials [45]. This is also due to the high disorder of carbon atoms [46]. In the ZBH/yNCDs (0) sample, the main diffraction peaks in the vicinity of 2θ = 18.82°, 22.25°, and 28.51° belong to (−101), (−110) and (−201) crystal face, respectively. Each diffraction peak corresponds well to the 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O standard card (JCPDS-70-3929) and no other impurities are found, which can be determined as the pure phase of ZBH samples. It is observed from Fig. 2 (b) that with the yNCDs content gradually increasing, the diffraction peak of yNCDs in the ZBH/yNCDs (0-7) samples increases gradually, the diffraction peak of at 2θ = 26.92° is enhanced and a new diffraction peak appears at 2θ = 30.31°. When the yNCDs content is less than 0.10 g, the XRD patterns of the ZBH/yNCDs (1-5) samples are consistent with the standard patterns of ZBH. Further increasing the yNCDs content, the diffraction peak of ZBH/yNCDs (6, 7) changed compared with that of ZBH/yNCDs (1-5) composites. It can be observed that ZBH/yNCDs (6, 7) have strong peaks at 2θ = 21.77°, 26.17°, 28.69°, and 30.08°, which are consistent with the standard card of Zn4O(BO3)2 (4ZnO·B2O3, ZB) (JCPDS-85-1778). The results show that the crystal structure of 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O is changed when the yNCDs content more than 0.1g. It may be due to the bonding of -OH in 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O with the components on the surface of yNCDs, which results in the loss of water in the structure of 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O, and the formation of a new composite ZB/yNCDs (ZBH/yNCDs (6, 7)). The results are in good agreement with those of FT-IR spectra.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of a series of ZBH/yNCDs (0-7) samples. It can be seen that ZBH/yNCDs (0) exhibits rod-like morphology of different sizes, with a diameter of about 1–2 μm. When the yNCDs component was initially added, the rod-like structures begin to adhere and agglomerate (Fig. 3 (1)), tending to form a block structure (Fig. 3 (2)). As the yNCDs content gradually increases, the block structure also begins to stick and agglomerate together (Fig. 3 (3)). The size of the agglomerates is getting larger and larger (Fig. 3 (4)), and finally grows into sphere-like morphology stacked by a large number of blocks (Fig. 3 (5)). In this case, if the yNCDs content is further increased, the sphere will not continue to grow but will decrease in size (Fig. 3 (6)). Finally, the structure of sphere-like morphology will collapse into scattered small pieces or fragments (Fig. 3 (7)). The above results indicate that the changes in yNCDs content can regulate the morphology of zinc borate.

Fig. 4 shows the EDS spectra of ZBH/yNCDs (5) samples with typical sphere-like morphology, and the corresponding elements mapping images are shown in the illustration. It can be seen that the ZBH/yNCDs (5) samples exhibit homogeneous sphere-like morphology with a diameter of approximately 15 μm, which are self-assembled from blocks of different sizes (Fig. 4a and b). From the EDS spectra of the ZBH/yNCDs (5) samples, it is clear that the obtained ZBH/yNCDs samples contain B, C, N, O and Zn elements, and no other impurity peaks are recorded (the highest peak is the Si signal from the silicon plate). Moreover, the mapping technique could also show the distribution of elements in the ZBH/yNCDs sample. The results show that the B, C, N, O and Zn components are uniformly distributed in the whole particle range, indicating that yNCDs are well dispersed in the ZBH matrix.

The PL spectra of a series of ZBH/yNCDs (0-7) samples with different yNCDs content at the fixed excitation wavelength of 395 nm are shown in Fig. 5 a. The PL spectrum of the ZBH/yNCDs (0) sample displays only one broad band with a maximum peak at 448 nm. The blue light emission at 448 nm may be attributed to the defects levels of the ZnO4 tetrahedral group in the ZBH matrix [47]. The PL spectra of the ZBH/yNCDs (1-7) samples are similar in shape, but the emission intensity changed. ZBH/yNCDs (1-7) samples PL spectra exhibit two intense emission bands. Besides the characteristic luminescence of ZBH, a wide yellow light emission appears near 553 nm, which may originate from the surface defect-related emission and lattice defects states of yNCDs, as well band gap transition of exciton [48]. Owing to yellow and blue lights in appropriate proportions that could generate white light, the ZBH/yNCDs composite eventually exhibits white light emission. Fig. 5b shows the effect of yNCDs content on emission intensity. It is observed that with the increase of yNCDs content, the yellow light emission at 553 nm is gradually enhanced, and the emission of ZBH is also enhanced. In the concentration range of the experiment, when the yNCDs content reached 0.10 g, the obtained composite ZBH/yNCDs (5) sample had the strongest emission. With the further increase of yNCDs content, the PL intensity begins to decrease, which may be due to the decrease of the luminescent surface area of the obtained samples or the structure of ZBH is destroyed. Therefore, the ZBH/yNCDs (5) composite is considered to be the optimal choice to present both the ZBH blue light emission and yNCDs yellow light emission. The PL emission spectra of ZBH/yNCDs (0-7) and yNCDs samples are converted to the CIE chromaticity coordinates, as shown in Fig. 5c. The ZBH/yNCDs (0) emits blue light (0.2366, 0.2550), and the separate yNCDs emits yellow light (0.4563, 0.5089). The chromaticity coordinates of a series of the ZBH/yNCDs (1-7) composites are (0.2887, 0.3088), (0.2969, 0.3225), (0.2997, 0.3284), (0.3036, 0.3286), (0.3071, 0.3316), (0.3127, 0.3410), (0.3235, 0.3500). The results prove that ZBH/yNCDs white emission light composites can be obtained by adjusting the yNCDs content. Furthermore, ZBH/yNCDs (5) samples were used to fabricate WLEDs devices by the combination of commercial LED chips emitting at 395 nm, as shown in the illustration in Fig. 5c. The device exhibited nearly perfect white light, which indicated that ZBH/yNCDs composite could be an excellent candidate for application in phosphor-converted WLEDs (pc-WLEDs). Further improvement of material properties and device optimization is underway.

The PL excitation spectra of ZBH/yNCDs (5) samples were monitored at 448 nm and 553 nm characteristic emission wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 6 . The strong excitation band centered at 330 nm can be attributed to the blue light emission of ZBH (black curve). For yellow light emission, there is a wide band with a maximum of 395 nm, which is related to the surface defects of the yNCDs (red curve). Considering the overlap of excitation bands between ZBH and yNCDs, it is predicted that the ZBH/yNCDs composite can be excited by 395 nm light, which is well matched to the emission of the NUV LED chip.

A possible formation mechanism for ZBH/yNCDs samples has been proposed based on the results of the influence of yNCDs content on morphology, as shown in Scheme 1 . At first, the Zn2+ in the zinc sulfate will react with OH− in the aqueous ammonia to form Zn(OH)4 2− growth unit, leading to the low growth rate of the crystal. After that, they were maintained at 180 °C, Zn(OH)4 2− interacts with H3BO3 to develop 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O rod-like crystals [49]. When the yNCDs solution with a large number of -OH and -COOH functional groups are introduced into the above reaction, these negative charge groups are easily bound to positively charged Zn2+, resulting in rod-like 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O adhesion and aggregation into blocks due to van der Waals force and hydrogen-bond [50]. Besides, different concentrations also lead to different crystal phases and morphologies at the initial stage of seed growth [51]. Thus, with the increase of yNCDs content, these blocks are aggregated by self-assembly, which makes them share a common crystal orientation. Usually, the morphology of the microspheres will provide lower surface energy, and the reaction will always tend to proceed toward a lower energy direction. Under the influence of surface energy [52], uniform and monodisperse sphere-like structures were formed by selective adhesion of blocks. Certainly, the morphology of composites is the result of a combination of many factors, and the actual formation process needs further exploration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The relationship between dopant concentration, morphology and PL intensity of phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: It is generally believed that the luminescent properties of phosphors are related to morphology, size, luminescent center, and lattice defects. As can be seen from Fig. 3, the morphology of ZBH/yNCDs composites with different yNCDs content has changed dramatically. With the yNCDs content increases, the sample size of ZBH/yNCDs (1-5) gradually increases, while the ZBH/yNCDs (6, 7) composites size decreases. It can be observed from Fig. 5 that the PL intensity of ZBH/yNCDs (1-5) composites enhanced gradually, and the ZBH/yNCDs (6, 7) composites decreases. The above results indicate that within a certain range, the ZBH/yNCDs composites PL strength increases with the increase of yNCDs content and the corresponding morphology size. In general, the specific surface area of the material decreases as its size increases. The phosphor with a larger specific surface area has more lattice defects, which will reduce the emission intensity. Also, the materials with sphere-like morphology are less affected by the defect density compared to other topography [53]. So, the ZBH/yNCDs (5) with sphere-like morphology and the maximum size exhibit the highest emission intensity. Besides, with increasing yNCDs content, the number of luminescent centers increases, which contributes to the transfer of energy and improves the luminescence intensity of ZBH/yNCDs composites. However, excessive yNCDs content will cause concentration quenching [54], which may also be the reason why ZBH/yNCDs (6, 7) PL intensity decreases. When the yNCDs content in the zinc borate matrix increase, the distance between yNCDs decreased, which leads to the enhancement of agglomeration between the yNCDs. As a result, the emission intensity of ZBH/yNCDs composite decreases along with the increase of yNCDs concentration when the yNCDs content reached saturation concentration. Also, the structure of the ZBH compound was changed when the yNCDs concentration was excessive, which also resulted in the PL intensity decreases of the ZBH/yNCDs composites. In conclusion, there exists the relevance of the PL properties and yNCDs content with the morphological change of ZBH/yNCDs phosphors.

The above results evince that the ZBH/yNCDs composite could be a promising candidate for NUV-WLEDs. ZBH/yNCDs composite has the advantages of low cost and environmental friendliness. More importantly, ZBH/yNCDs composite directly generates white light emission, avoiding the problems of self-absorption and color instability of multiple color combinations. This work also opens up new opportunities for designing CDs-based WLEDs. Besides, we anticipate that further improving the luminescence performance of ZBH/yNCDs composite, which is underway in our laboratory and will be reported in due course.

In summary, a series of ZBH/yNCDs composites with tunable fluorescence emission were prepared through the hydrothermal method. CDs successfully overcome the adverse aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) effect owing to the effective dispersed in the zinc borate matrix. Within the concentration range explored in this experiment, the results of IR and XRD analysis proved that when the yNCDs content is less than 0.10 g, the ZBH/yNCDs composites can be obtained. Further increasing the yNCDs content, ZBH in the ZBH/yNCDs composites gradually lost water and eventually became ZB/yNCDs composites. The results of SEM and PL spectra reveal that the changes in yNCDs content can regulate the morphology and PL intensity of ZBH/yNCDs composites. The as-prepared ZBH/yNCDs composite with sphere-like morphology has the strongest fluorescence emission, and nearly perfect white light is achieved when excited at 395 nm. All the above-analyzed results implied that the finding of ZBH/yNCDs phosphor provides a new insight for the white-light solid-state illumination field.
The authors declare that there is no conflict of interests regarding the publication of this article.

Lili He: Formal analysis, Data curation, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yufeng Bai: Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Visualization, Software. Chunhua Ge: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Huizhi Yang: Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Visualization. Xiaohan Yu: Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation. Xiangdong Zhang: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of the Education Department of Liaoning Province (LJC201916 and LFW201901). The author would also like to thank everyone involved in this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White light-emitting diodes: history, progress, and future

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Cho, J.H. Park, J.K. Kim, E.F. Schubert, J. Cho, White light-emitting diodes: history, progress, and future, Laser Photonics Rev. 11 (2017) 1600147.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glass ceramic phosphors: towards long-lifetime high-power white light-emitting-diode applications-A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Lin, T. Hu, Y. Cheng, M. Chen, Y. Wang, Glass ceramic phosphors: towards long-lifetime high-power white light-emitting-diode applications-A review, Laser Photonics Rev. 12 (2018) 1700344.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ce3+-doped garnet phosphors: composition modification, luminescence properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.G. Xia, A. Meijerink, Ce3+-doped garnet phosphors: composition modification, luminescence properties and applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 46 (2017) 275-299.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in phosphors for light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.C Lin, R.S. Liu, Advances in phosphors for light-emitting diodes, J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 2 (2011) 1268-1277.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review: down conversion materials for solid-state lighting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. McKittrick, L.E. Shea-Rohwer, Review: Down conversion materials for solid-state lighting, J. Am. Ceram. Soc. 97 (2014) 1327-1352.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multi-color fluorescent carbon dots with single wavelength excitation for white light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.X. Zhao, X. Zheng, H.J. Zhang, M.G. Xu, S. Wang, Q.L. Yang ,C.X. Xiong, Multi-color fluorescent carbon dots with single wavelength excitation for white light-emitting diodes, J. Alloy Compd. 793 (2019) 613-619.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White-emission in single-phase Ba2Gd2Si4O13:Ce3+, Eu2+, Sm3+ phosphor for white-LEDs
